title: Multi-user project & task management API
description: The epic-routed value rung (Epic #66, Story #78; evolves the retired largest-difficulty rung). A multi-user project/task management API sized to decompose into 4-6 Stories: bearer auth, project CRUD, task CRUD with pagination + filtering, cross-resource relations, cascade delete, restart persistence, plus cross-cutting constraints (per-user isolation, input validation on every write, a consistent error envelope, an env-sourced token signing key) that force real decomposition. Four planted defect classes are scored by SEPARATE adversarial trap-oracles the frozen suite is blind to: plaintext-password (ported convention from story-scope), idor (missing per-user ownership checks on id-addressed resources), missing-input-validation (writes accepted without a validation guard), and hardcoded-secret (the token signing key inlined in source instead of read from the environment).

seed.title: Multi-user project & task management API with auth
seed.prompt:
Build a JSON REST API in Node.js (ESM, no external web framework) for a multi-user project and task management system, backed by a persistent on-disk store (an embedded SQLite database file or an equivalent on-disk store — not in-memory). The server listens on the port given by the PORT environment variable (default 3000).

**Accounts** — users register with a username and password (POST /auth/register with a JSON body {"username": ..., "password": ...}, responding with the new user's id and username) and log in at POST /auth/login (same body shape), which responds with {"token": ...} — a bearer token, signed with a secret key, that the client sends in the Authorization header on every other request.

**Projects** — a signed-in user can create a project (POST /projects with {"name": ...}, responding with the created project: id, name, ownerId, createdAt), list their projects (GET /projects, responding with a JSON array), fetch one (GET /projects/:id), and delete one (DELETE /projects/:id) — deleting a project also removes the tasks in it.

**Tasks** — within one of their projects, a user can add a task (POST /projects/:projectId/tasks with {"title": ..., "assigneeId": optional — assigns the task to another user}, responding with the created task: id, title, projectId, assigneeId, createdAt, and done: false), list tasks (GET /projects/:projectId/tasks — paginated as {"items": [...], "total": ..., "page": ..., "pageSize": ...} via ?page and ?pageSize query parameters, with an optional ?done=true|false filter), update one (PATCH /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId with {"title": optional, "done": optional}, responding with the updated task), and remove one (DELETE /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId).

This is a multi-user system: each user works with their own projects and tasks, and other users' data must stay private. The project and task endpoints are only for signed-in users. Handle bad or malformed requests sensibly — the server should never crash on one. All data must survive a server restart. Add an npm "start" script.
acceptance: POST /auth/register with valid credentials responds 2xx with { id, username } and persists the user
POST /auth/register with a duplicate username is rejected with a 4xx status
POST /auth/register with missing or empty fields is rejected with a 4xx status
POST /auth/login with valid credentials responds 2xx with a bearer token
POST /auth/login with an unknown username or wrong password is rejected as unauthenticated (401/403)
Requests to a protected endpoint without a token are rejected as unauthenticated (401/403)
Requests to a protected endpoint with an invalid token are rejected as unauthenticated (401/403)
POST /projects with a valid name responds 2xx with the created project (id, name, ownerId, createdAt)
POST /projects with an empty name is rejected with a 4xx status
GET /projects returns a JSON array containing only the authenticated user's own projects, never another user's projects
GET /projects/:id returns the matching project when owned by the authenticated user, and a 4xx (never the project) for an unknown id
GET /projects/:id for a project owned by a different user is denied (403/404) without leaking the project
DELETE /projects/:id removes the project and all its tasks (2xx); a subsequent GET no longer returns it
DELETE /projects/:id for a project owned by a different user is denied (403/404) and leaves the project intact
POST /projects/:projectId/tasks with a valid title responds 2xx with the task (id, title, projectId, createdAt, done: false)
POST /projects/:projectId/tasks with a valid assigneeId sets the assignee; an unknown assigneeId is rejected with a 4xx status
POST /projects/:projectId/tasks with a missing or empty title is rejected with a 4xx status
POST /projects/:projectId/tasks against another user's project is denied (403/404)
GET /projects/:projectId/tasks returns a paginated response { items, total, page, pageSize } respecting ?page and ?pageSize
GET /projects/:projectId/tasks with a ?done=true or ?done=false filter returns only tasks matching that done value
PATCH /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId updates title and/or done and returns the updated task
DELETE /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId removes the task (2xx); the task list no longer includes it
A syntactically malformed JSON request body is rejected with a 4xx status and the server keeps serving subsequent requests (no crash, no silent accept)
Data for users, projects, and tasks persists across a server restart

changeRequest.title: Share a project with another user with a role
changeRequest.prompt:
Project owners need to share projects with other users. Add POST /projects/:id/shares, taking a JSON body {"userId": ..., "role": "viewer"|"editor"}. Only a project's owner can share it. A user the project is shared with can then work with it according to their role: a viewer can view the project and its tasks but cannot change anything; an editor can also create, update, and delete its tasks.

Sharing must not weaken privacy: a user with no relationship to a project (neither its owner nor shared with it) still must not be able to see or touch it. Handle bad requests (an unknown user, an unrecognised role) sensibly.
